target: WIN-061 detail_evidence in data/wins.json — single targeted substring patch resolving the H_eff-averaging-method carry-forward hole (c4 H8 / c3 H7 / ISS-1454). Inserts a brief disclosure that (a) the H_eff ≈ 3,400 km figure is the simple linear average of H(r) over the disc from r = 0 to R = 20,015 km, (b) alternative averaging choices (area-weighted, pole-only) also overshoot 7.83 Hz in different directions, and (c) a strict upper-bound argument — H(r) peaks at H(0) = 8,537 km, so the minimum possible first-mode frequency from this H(r) is c/(4·8,537 km) = 8.79 Hz > 7.83 Hz — forecloses the cherry-pick objection entirely by ruling out every possible averaging choice in one inequality. The ~22 Hz figure is preserved; the 7.83 Hz observed baseline is preserved; the subsequent "The model cannot explain the normal-day frequency" sentence is preserved verbatim. No TLDR or detail_verdict_text changes.
source_issue: ISS-1454 (moderate, carry-forward from c3 H7; c4 H8) — H_eff averaging method still undisclosed in preserved baseline text. Three averaging methods (simple-linear: 22.6 Hz; area-weighted: 35.1 Hz; pole-only: 8.79 Hz) all overshoot 7.83 Hz, but text omits method disclosure. Dome defender can claim cherry-picking; trivial to refute but not in text.
patch_instructions: Single substring-replacement patch in WIN-061 detail_evidence in data/wins.json. The before-string is verified unique (occurrence count = 1) as of this EXP creation timestamp. After integration, verify: (a) the "~22 Hz" opening figure and the 7.83 Hz observed baseline sentence are preserved verbatim; (b) the immediately following sentence "The model cannot explain the normal-day frequency, so claiming to explain its storm-time suppression is building on a foundation that doesn't exist." is unchanged; (c) the "H_eff ≈ 3,400 km" anchor phrase still appears (used elsewhere in the text? it appears once in the current field — after this patch it still appears once, now inside the expanded parenthetical); (d) all UTF-8 scientific characters are preserved: ≈, →, ·, ≥ — do NOT auto-convert to ASCII. Character delta: before = 138 chars, after = 628 chars, net +490 chars. Projected new detail_evidence length: 5138 + 490 = 5628 chars. TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are not modified by this EXP — the new parenthetical is factually consistent with the existing "~22 Hz baseline" TLDR framing and merely adds the averaging-method disclosure beneath it.

patch_anchors.before: predicts a baseline Schumann frequency of ~22 Hz (using H_eff ≈ 3,400 km). The observed baseline is 7.83 Hz.
patch_anchors.after: predicts a baseline Schumann frequency of ~22 Hz (using H_eff ≈ 3,400 km — the simple linear average of H(r) across the disc from r = 0 to R = 20,015 km; the averaging method is not a cherry-pick: area-weighted averaging (by disc area 2πr dr) gives H_eff ≈ 2,135 km → ~35 Hz (farther from 7.83 Hz), and the pole-only maximum H(0) = 8,537 km gives the lowest possible frequency from this H(r), ~8.8 Hz — still above observed. Because 7.83 Hz requires H_eff ≥ 9,579 km and H(r) peaks at 8,537 km, no averaging choice can reach the observed baseline). The observed baseline is 7.83 Hz.
patch_anchors.rationale: ISS-1454 (c4 H8 / c3 H7, moderate): the preserved baseline-argument sentence quotes "H_eff ≈ 3,400 km" as a choice without telling the reader which average produced it. Three reasonable averaging methods over the dome H(r) exponential disc profile give different numbers — simple-linear average ≈ 3,316 km → 22.6 Hz, area-weighted (by disc area) ≈ 2,135 km → 35.1 Hz, pole-only (using H(0) = 8,537 km as the lowest-frequency bound) → 8.79 Hz — and ALL three overshoot the observed 7.83 Hz. A sophisticated dome defender can argue "which average? if you picked simple-linear deliberately to maximize the mismatch, you cherry-picked the method." The rebuttal is trivial (no method works), but it is not in the text and therefore cannot be invoked in a reader-visible way. The patch closes the hole in two moves: (1) discloses that H_eff ≈ 3,400 km is the simple linear average over the disc from r=0 to r=R=20,015 km — removing ambiguity about which method was used; (2) includes the alternative-method numbers (area-weighted ~35 Hz, pole-only ~8.8 Hz) explicitly, showing that each miss the 7.83 Hz target in different directions but none hit it. Beyond the curmudgeon-recommended disclosure, the patch adds a strict upper-bound argument: because the cavity formula H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) km peaks at H(0) = 8,537 km, any disc-integrated average satisfies H_eff ≤ 8,537 km, so the minimum possible first-mode frequency is f_min = c/(4·H_max) = 300,000/(4·8,537) = 8.79 Hz. Since 7.83 Hz < 8.79 Hz, no averaging scheme of any kind — uniform, area-weighted, volume-weighted, mode-weighted, path-weighted, or otherwise — can reach the observed baseline. This converts the cherry-pick defense from "we checked three and they all fail" (an inductive claim) to "there exists no averaging method that can succeed" (a deductive bound), which is a substantially stronger closure of the objection. The sentence structure preserves the original "(using H_eff ≈ 3,400 km). The observed baseline is 7.83 Hz." rhythm by extending the parenthetical and re-closing with the same sentence boundary.

holes_addressed.hole: c4 H8 / c3 H7 / ISS-1454 (moderate, carry-forward — H_eff averaging method undisclosed)
holes_addressed.description: The baseline-argument sentence "predicts a baseline Schumann frequency of ~22 Hz (using H_eff ≈ 3,400 km)" does not specify which averaging method over the dome disc produces H_eff = 3,400 km. Simple-linear gives 3,316 km → 22.6 Hz; area-weighted gives 2,135 km → 35.1 Hz; pole-only gives 8,537 km → 8.79 Hz. All three overshoot the observed 7.83 Hz, so the Self-Contradicted verdict is robust to method choice — but the text does not tell the reader this. A defender can accuse us of cherry-picking simple-linear to inflate the mismatch (22.6 Hz vs 7.83 Hz is a 2.9× overshoot; pole-only gives 8.79 Hz vs 7.83 Hz, only a 12% overshoot). Without the disclosure in-text, the rebuttal is unavailable at the point of reading.
holes_addressed.fix: Expand the parenthetical to disclose (1) that H_eff ≈ 3,400 km is the simple linear average of H(r) over the disc from r=0 to R=20,015 km; (2) that area-weighted averaging gives ≈ 2,135 km → ~35 Hz, farther from 7.83 Hz; (3) that pole-only (maximum H) gives the lowest-possible frequency, ~8.8 Hz — still above observed; and (4) a strict upper-bound argument: H(r) peaks at 8,537 km, so H_eff ≤ 8,537 km, so f ≥ c/(4·8,537) = 8.79 Hz > 7.83 Hz for ANY averaging choice. This moves the rebuttal from inductive ("three methods checked, all fail") to deductive ("no method can succeed — proven by bound"), forestalling the cherry-pick objection decisively.

new_evidence_added: Simple-linear averaging explicitly identified as the method behind H_eff ≈ 3,400 km. Computation: (1/R)·∫_0^R H(r) dr = (H_0/R)·a·(1 − exp(−R/a)) with H_0=8,537 km, a=8,619 km, R=20,015 km → H_eff ≈ 3,316 km → f ≈ 22.6 Hz. The text rounds to "3,400 km" for readability (the current value in wins.json) — the exact figure 3,316 km is consistent and the rounding is immaterial to the argument.

Area-weighted averaging result: H_eff ≈ 2,135 km → ~35 Hz. Computation: (2/R²)·∫_0^R r·H(r) dr = (2·H_0/R²)·[a² − (a² + a·R)·exp(−R/a)] with the same parameters → H_eff ≈ 2,134.8 km → f ≈ 35.13 Hz. This is the disc-area-weighted version of "average cavity height" when the relevant observable weights heights by surface area, and gives a LARGER frequency than simple-linear because weighting by area favors the disc periphery where H(r) is smaller.

Pole-only (maximum H) result: H(0) = 8,537 km → f = c/(4·H_0) = 300,000/(4·8,537) = 8.79 Hz. This is the LOWEST POSSIBLE first-mode frequency from the dome H(r) — achievable only if one uses the pole value as H_eff and ignores the rest of the disc. Still above the observed 7.83 Hz.

Strict upper-bound argument: H_max = H(0) = 8,537 km for the dome H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) km. Any averaging of a function over its domain gives a value ≤ its maximum, so H_eff ≤ 8,537 km for every reasonable averaging scheme. Therefore f_min = c/(4·H_max) = 8.79 Hz is a strict lower bound on the first-mode Schumann frequency derivable from this H(r). Since 7.83 Hz < 8.79 Hz, no averaging choice can reach the observed baseline. This deductively forecloses the cherry-pick objection without needing to enumerate every possible averaging method.

anticipated_objections.objection: The c3 review quoted "H_eff ≈ 3,400 km" but the computed simple-linear value is 3,316 km. The patch text says the H_eff = 3,400 km figure IS the simple-linear average but then cites 3,316 km implicitly in the arithmetic. That is internally inconsistent.
anticipated_objections.response: Fair concern. The existing wins.json text says "H_eff ≈ 3,400 km" (a rounded figure) and the patch preserves that value rather than introducing a second number. The rounding from 3,316 to 3,400 changes the frequency from 22.6 to 22.1 Hz — both within the "~22 Hz" band quoted in the text. The patch uses the tilde (≈) to signal the rounding. If the decider prefers the exact value, replace "3,400 km" with "3,316 km" everywhere in the sentence; the "~22 Hz" headline is unaffected either way. Either choice is defensible; not changing the existing 3,400-km figure is less intrusive.

anticipated_objections.objection: The upper-bound argument requires that the "averaging" producing H_eff be bounded by H_max. This is true for arithmetic, area-weighted, and any positive-weight linear average — but a non-linear transformation (e.g., harmonic mean, or using some dome-defined weighting like exp(+λr)) could in principle yield a different number. The strict-bound claim may overreach.
anticipated_objections.response: Legitimate subtlety. The strict upper bound on H_eff ≤ H_max holds for any convex combination / positive-weight linear average (because a weighted average cannot exceed the max value of the averaged quantity). It does not hold for arbitrary non-linear transformations — for example, a harmonic mean of H(r) would give a value ≤ arithmetic mean, further INCREASING the predicted frequency (worse for the dome). What COULD in principle give a value > H_max is a transformation that adds to H(r) — e.g., "H_eff = H_max + boundary_term" — but such a transformation is not an averaging of H(r) itself and would require a separate physical justification. The patch uses "no averaging choice" rather than "no transformation" deliberately, to confine the claim to the mathematically rigorous class (positive-weight linear averages). If the dome invents an H_eff that exceeds H_max via a non-averaging mechanism, that mechanism would be the new testable claim — not a cherry-pick of averaging method.

anticipated_objections.objection: The patch adds ~490 chars to an already 5138-char detail_evidence field. The total becomes 5628 chars, which is at the upper end of WIN-field length and may degrade readability. The cherry-pick objection is moderate severity; does a ~10% length increase justify it?
anticipated_objections.response: Length concern is real. Options considered: (a) minimal form — disclose only that 3,400 km is the simple-linear average (adds ~80 chars), but leaves the cherry-pick surface open to alternative-method invocation; (b) medium form — disclose method plus the two alternative-method numbers (adds ~300 chars), closes the cherry-pick attack inductively; (c) current form — method + alternatives + deductive bound (adds ~490 chars), closes the attack deductively. Selected form (c) because the deductive bound is a one-inequality closure that is more durable than enumeration — it handles any future averaging choice a defender might invent without requiring a further revision. If the decider prefers shorter, form (b) is available: drop the final two clauses starting with "Because 7.83 Hz requires H_eff ≥ 9,579 km" — saves ~140 chars and keeps the inductive version. Form (a) is a further step down and is not recommended because it leaves the inductive surface open.

anticipated_objections.objection: The "H_eff ≥ 9,579 km" threshold comes from requiring f = c/(4·H_eff) = 7.83 Hz, so H_eff = 300,000/(4·7.83) = 9578.5 km. The text rounds to 9,579. The reader has to do this back-of-envelope calculation to see why 9,579 km is the relevant threshold — adding a one-clause gloss ("required to match observed frequency") would help.
anticipated_objections.response: Good usability point. The current patch text includes the reasoning inline: "Because 7.83 Hz requires H_eff ≥ 9,579 km and H(r) peaks at 8,537 km" — implicit arithmetic is c/(4·f) = 300,000/(4·7.83) ≈ 9,579 km. A more reader-friendly form would be: "Because matching the 7.83 Hz observed baseline would require H_eff ≥ c/(4·7.83 Hz) = 9,579 km and H(r) peaks at 8,537 km". Adds ~20 chars; marginal. Decider judgment call — either form works.

anticipated_objections.objection: A fair-minded dome defender would still argue that our H_eff ≤ H_max bound assumes the first-mode SR frequency formula f = c/(4·H) holds in the dome model at all. If the dome model redefines the SR frequency formula (e.g., f = c/(α·H) with some α ≠ 4, or f = function of H and some aetheric parameter), the bound might not translate directly to 8.79 Hz.
anticipated_objections.response: True, but the paragraph as a whole already pins down the formula: the original text derives ~22 Hz from H_eff = 3,400 km using f = c/(4·H) implicitly — the dome's own cavity formula uses this standard first-mode Schumann expression, otherwise the ~22 Hz figure in dome source material would be different. If the dome redefines the frequency formula to rescue the 7.83 Hz match, that itself is a post-hoc rescue and is separately refutable. The current text does not need to anticipate every possible future redefinition; it needs to close the cherry-pick objection on the ACTUAL formula the dome uses. That is what the patch does. Any formula redefinition is a new claim subject to new testing — a separate curmudgeon/analyst cycle, not this one.

kernel_of_truth: The c4 curmudgeon recommendation correctly identifies that the cherry-pick objection is trivially refutable because all three reasonable averaging methods overshoot 7.83 Hz — this is an honest kernel: yes, the dome could claim "you picked simple-linear to maximize the mismatch", and yes, the response is "we checked, no reasonable method works". The patch takes this kernel and strengthens it: instead of an enumerative rebuttal (which remains open to invoking an unchecked method), the patch supplies a deductive bound from the maximum value of H(r), which closes the class of all positive-weight averages at once. The original curmudgeon suggested text — "simple-linear average; area-weighted gives 35 Hz; pole-only gives 8.8 Hz; no reasonable averaging choice recovers the observed baseline" — is preserved and reinforced by the bound argument, not replaced.
why_it_doesnt_save_claim: This patch does not change the Self-Contradicted verdict or weaken any existing argument. The ~22 Hz baseline overshoot (H_eff ≈ 3,400 km using simple-linear averaging) is preserved as the headline figure. The WIN-038 cross-reference (pole-only floor at 8.78 Hz) is consistent with the patch (pole-only value 8.79 Hz — 0.01 Hz roundoff). The new material strengthens the Self-Contradicted verdict against a class of methodological defenses that was previously not addressed in-text. The dome still has no formula that derives the claimed ≥30% suppression threshold or 6-hour window from H(r), H_0, or λ_g, and the Live Power Dashboard "Unscored" contradiction and the PRED-SR-SUPPRESS post-storm registration timeline (both still open in other EXPs) remain untouched. The baseline-overshoot attack is now method-independent, which is a strictly stronger position.
cross_references: monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-061.c4.json — c4 H8 (this EXP's target; also c3 H7 carry-forward). The c4 review recommended: "Separate EXP, small footnote-style addition to paragraph 1 baseline text: H_eff = 3,400 km is the simple linear average of H(r) over the disc from 0 to R = 20,015 km; area-weighted averaging gives 35 Hz (farther from 7.83 Hz), pole-only gives 8.8 Hz (still above the observed 7.83 Hz). No reasonable averaging choice recovers the observed baseline." This EXP implements that recommendation and adds the deductive upper-bound argument.

data/wins.json WIN-038 (Schumann stability, verdict Self-Contradicted) — independently establishes the pole-only floor at 8.78 Hz (0.01 Hz roundoff from this EXP's 8.79 Hz value). The cross-WIN consistency is preserved: the bound argument in this EXP is the same physics as WIN-038's pole-only-floor argument, now made explicit in WIN-061 as well.

monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/WIN-061.c4.json quantitative_verification block — verified the three averaging-method numbers (simple-linear 22.62 Hz, area-weighted 35.1 Hz, pole-only 8.79 Hz) used in this patch.
unaddressed_holes_note: EXP-233 scope is strictly ISS-1454 (c4 H8 / c3 H7, moderate — H_eff averaging method disclosure in the preserved baseline sentence). The c4 review flagged two additional items that remain open and are explicitly OUT OF SCOPE for this EXP: (a) ISS-1418 / c3 H5 (major — PRED-SR-SUPPRESS timestamp-forward registration argument still absent from WIN-061 text; registration_date 2026-03-23, 1-3 days after March 20-22 G3 storm; companion PRED-093 crypto-timestamped 2026-04-04, 13 days after same storm; this is the sharpest remaining attack and deserves its own EXP); (b) cross-WIN additions — WIN-038 stability-window tension and WIN-034 copper-firmament-conductivity tension, both flagged as "free wins" in the c4 stronger_arguments block. Recommend decider queue follow-up EXPs for (a) and consider curmudgeon-driven cross-WIN additions in a future review cycle. TLDRs and detail_verdict_text remain unchanged and consistent with the rewritten text after this patch.
notes_for_decider: Single atomic substring-replacement patch in WIN-061 detail_evidence. The before-string was verified unique (expected_occurrences: 1) at EXP creation time — quoted from current wins.json after the EXP-232 integration that brought the field length to 5138 chars. IMPORTANT character-set notes: the patch text uses the following UTF-8 scientific characters — ≈ (U+2248), → (U+2192), · (U+00B7), ≥ (U+2265). The "·" (middle dot) is used for multiplication in "2πr dr" and "c/(4·8,537)" to match the paragraph style. Do NOT auto-convert any of these to ASCII approximations (*, ->, =>, >=). Character delta: +490 chars net. Projected new detail_evidence length: 5628 chars (from 5138). After integration: (a) run tests; (b) verify the "~22 Hz" and "H_eff ≈ 3,400 km" anchor phrases still appear exactly once each; (c) verify the "The observed baseline is 7.83 Hz." sentence remains intact with its period; (d) verify the following sentence "The model cannot explain the normal-day frequency" is unchanged; (e) consider whether to similarly close c4 H3 / ISS-1418 (PRED-SR-SUPPRESS timestamp-forward registration) and the WIN-038 / WIN-034 cross-WIN stronger_arguments in subsequent EXPs. TLDRs and detail_verdict_text are NOT modified by this EXP. If the decider prefers a shorter form without the deductive bound (form (b) in anticipated_objections #3), drop the final two clauses starting with "Because 7.83 Hz requires H_eff ≥ 9,579 km..." and end the parenthetical at "still above observed". Saves ~140 chars, keeps the inductive rebuttal.
deeper_analysis:
ISS-1454 is a carry-forward hole surviving two curmudgeon cycles (c3 H7, c4 H8) because each successive EXP was narrowly scoped to a different problem — EXP-227 focused on S&F citation direction, EXP-232 focused on SPE/storm category deconflation, neither touched the H_eff averaging sentence. The curmudgeon c4 review correctly characterizes the severity: moderate, because the cherry-pick objection is trivial to refute but the rebuttal is not in-text; and correctly recommends a small footnote-style addition.

The interesting strategic question is whether to do the minimum patch (disclose method only) or the stronger patch (add the upper-bound argument). I chose the stronger form because the upper-bound argument converts an enumerative defense ("we checked three methods") into a deductive closure ("no method can succeed — proven by the maximum of H(r)"). The mathematical argument is clean: for any positive-weight linear average, H_eff ≤ H_max = H(0) = 8,537 km, so f = c/(4·H_eff) ≥ c/(4·8,537) = 8.79 Hz > 7.83 Hz. This is a one-inequality closure that handles every future averaging scheme a defender might invoke — harmonic means, path-integrals along specific rays, volume-weighted averages over some aetheric density, whatever — without needing a future revision.

Why the upper-bound matters for credibility: the dome text (and the Live Power Dashboard narrative) could at any time respond to our critique by inventing a new averaging prescription. If our response is only "we checked simple-linear, area-weighted, and pole-only", the defender has an infinite space of alternatives to probe. If our response is "H_eff cannot exceed H_max = 8,537 km; 7.83 Hz requires H_eff ≥ 9,579 km; no averaging scheme can cross the gap", the defender has nowhere to go within the class of averaging methods. This moves our rebuttal from a game where we must enumerate exhaustively to a game where we have closed the space.

The cost is ~490 chars and one additional inequality in the text. The curmudgeon c4 recommendation text is ~160 chars of direct suggestion; the full patch with the upper-bound adds ~330 chars beyond that. In exchange, the cherry-pick objection is decisively closed rather than merely refuted inductively. The verdict (Self-Contradicted) was never at risk from the cherry-pick attack, so the patch is not verdict-critical — it is credibility-surface polish that reduces the number of future review cycles needed to defend the baseline argument.

On the other side of the argument (user preference, applied): A sympathetic dome-side critic would argue: (A) the upper-bound argument is technically correct but pedantic — adding a rigorous mathematical bound to a polish issue is disproportionate, and a reader who already accepts the three-method enumeration does not need the bound. Response: true, the enumeration suffices for most readers; the bound is for the subset who would otherwise invoke a fourth method. The bound costs ~140 chars; whether that cost is worth paying is a judgment call, and the decider can trim if the extra material reads as over-engineering. (B) the bound assumes f = c/(4·H), which is a specific cavity-resonance formula; if the dome uses a different formula (e.g., with a geometric correction factor), the bound weakens. Response: the ~22 Hz figure in dome source material derives from the same formula, so the dome's own calculation accepts f = c/(4·H). If the dome redefines the formula to rescue the baseline, that is a separate post-hoc rescue subject to its own critique. (C) inserting a nested parenthetical with multiple "→" arrows degrades prose flow. Response: valid typographic point; the decider can re-punctuate the parenthetical into shorter sentences if preferred. The content is what matters; the form can be adjusted without losing the argument.

Alternative approaches considered and rejected: (1) Minimal disclosure only — adds method identification but leaves cherry-pick surface open to alternative-method invocation. Rejected because it shortens the patch by only ~140 chars at the cost of inductive-not-deductive closure. (2) Footnote-style addition at the end of the paragraph instead of inline parenthetical — rejected because the averaging-method question arises exactly at the "(using H_eff ≈ 3,400 km)" figure; locating the disclosure elsewhere creates reader friction. (3) Move to a separate subsection — rejected as out-of-proportion for a moderate hole; the inline parenthetical is the right scale. (4) Merge with other WIN-061 open holes (ISS-1418 timestamp-forward sentences, cross-WIN additions) into a larger EXP — rejected because scope discipline is a pipeline virtue and merging uncorrelated holes creates integration risk.

Overall assessment: this is a small, well-scoped patch that closes a carry-forward moderate hole decisively by adding both the curmudgeon-recommended disclosure and a slightly stronger upper-bound argument. The Self-Contradicted verdict is reinforced, not changed.
tldr_change: None. tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict currently frame the baseline argument around "~22 Hz baseline rather than the observed 7.83 Hz" and "the wrong baseline (~22 Hz vs 7.83 Hz)" — both statements remain true after this patch (the ~22 Hz figure is preserved as the simple-linear-average result). The averaging-method disclosure is detail-level content (which reader wants the full picture, not the punchline reader), and per analyst.md TLDR rule "Fix factual errors. Do not split hairs on nuance — the expanded detail handles that", this is below the revision threshold. No tldr changes recommended.
